title: The Bard Of Furthest Out
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He longed to be a Back-Blocks Bard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fame he wished to win,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He wrote at night and studied hard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (He read The Bulletin);
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sent in ן¿½stuffן¿½ unceasingly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But couldnן¿½t get it through;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so, at last, he came to me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see what I could do.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The poetן¿½s light was in his eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He aimed to be a man;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He bought a bluey and a fly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A brand new billy-can.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I showed him how to roll his swag
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½sling itן¿½ with the best;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I gave him my old water-bag,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pointed to the west.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now you can take the train as far
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As Blazes if you like,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wealthy go by motor-car
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Some travellers go by bike);
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They race it through without a rest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And find it very tame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But if you tramp it to the west
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½ll get there just the same.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½(No matter if the hour is late,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The morning goes Out-Back),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You do not need a dog nor mate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½ll find them on the track.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You must avoid such deadly rhymes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As ן¿½selfן¿½ and ן¿½elfן¿½ and ן¿½shelfן¿½.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But were it as in other times,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½d go with you myself.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Those days are done for me, but ah!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On hills where you shall be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wattle and the waratah
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are good to smell and see.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But thereן¿½s a scent, my heart believes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That ן¿½travellersן¿½ set higher
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than wattle, ן¿½tis the dried gum leaves
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That light the evening fire.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The evening fire and morning fire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are one fire in the Bush.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Youן¿½ll find the points that you require
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As towards the west you push.)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as you pass by ancient ways,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old camps, and mountain springs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The spirits of the Roaring Days
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will whisper many things.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The lonely ridge-and-gully belt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The spirit of the whole
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It must be seen; it must be felt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must sink into your soul!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The summer silence-creek-oaksן¿½ sigh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The windy, rainy ן¿½wooshן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis known to other men, and I,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Spirit of the Bush!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So on, and on, through dust and heat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When past the spurs you be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you shall meet whom you shall meet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And see what you shall see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You need not claim the strangerן¿½s due,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They yield it everywhere,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mateship is a thing that you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must take for granted there.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And in the land of Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Right up and furthest out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You find a new Australia there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That we know nought about.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Live as they live, fight as they fight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Succeed as they succeed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then come back again and write
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For all the world to read.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve got a note from Hungerford,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis written frank and fair;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bushmanן¿½s grim philosophy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bushmanן¿½s grin are there.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tramping on through rain and drought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unlooked for and unmissed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I may have sent to furthest out
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Great Bush Novelist.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
